New comment. Things that occurs to me whilst playing with modules...
```
      cache_timeout: refresh interval for this module (default 2)
 +    catnip_timeout: refresh interval for this module (default 60)
```

Say, I don't always play`gpmdp`, `cmus`, `clementine`, `mpris`, `spotify`, `deadbeef`, `player_control`, etc... often so this default timeout might seem silly (2 sec) (or 5 sec) nonstop for most of them. Other modules are like that too. We're only using one `cache_timeout` for almost everything. `catnip_timeout` goes right under `cache_timeout`.

Is it more efficient to place certain `catnip` in some modules? If `gpmdp` is not started, use `catnip_timeout (60)` until it's started, then `cache_timeout (2)`. If `60` is too much, maybe `30` or `15`? `15` is still better than `2*7`. Thoughts? Useful or useless optimization?

I prefer the relative imports. It allows for shorter lines and easier migrations of code. Plus, it's less repetition. Plus, why not take advantage of the clearer context of the module name?

```
from . import file_base
```

Then use `file_base.Keyring` and `file_base.decodebytes` below. It'll be a nicer looking import section plus code below that's easier to recognize the context (without having to read the import section). Moreover, it gives the advantage of being able to leverage the namespace, so if you have code here that presents the same concept, it can re-use the name (such as where [setuptools overrides sdist](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/sdist.py#L24)).

@rhyshort so looking at this again my current change means there is no behaviour change from existing.

Adding dbcopy in a view:
`ddoc.add_view(view_name='myview', map_func='', reduce_func='', dbcopy='mycopy')`

This stores the dbcopy parameter in the view (which is the old Cloudant location) and it is accessible via: `ddoc['views']['myview']['dbcopy']`
If the ddoc is saved to older version of Cloudant it stays there. 
If it is saved to a CouchDB 2.0 version the **server** performs a transform that moves the dbcopy into the `{'options' : {'epi' : {'dbcopy' : {'myview' : 'mycopy'}}}` location.

When a design document is retrieved if a `dbcopy` parameter exists in the old location it remains there so is still accessible via `ddoc['views']['myview']['dbcopy']`
If the dbcopy parameter is in the new location the de-transform I added references it from the old location as well so it is accessible from both:
`ddoc['views']['myview']['dbcopy']`
and
`ddoc['options']['epi']['dbcopy']['myview']`

I think there is a slight hole with this approach in that when creating a view using the old style `dbcopy` kwarg it is not accessible in the new location until after a save/fetch with remote. However, it it still possible to add the `dbcopy` directly in the new location through the design document's dictionary. I feel like someone adding `dbcopy` in the old way wouldn't be looking for it in the new location anyway.

Does that make things more clear?
Are you suggesting that we should not make `ddoc['views']['myview']['dbcopy']` accessible unless specifically asked for?

However, `git` only displays the username and password prompts when it has no credentials to fill, so what we do here instead is pass a single newline. If guy has credentials to fill it will quit and then exit. If it doesn't, it will block waiting for the username and password. If git hasn't quit after 1 second , we assume we're in the latter case and deal with that appropriately.

A few points about this bit.

1. the logic here is a bit wrong.  You recompute `_h_tani_over_r`, `_r0_cosi`, and `_inv_r0_cosi` when `i` is within the allowed limits, so the calculation you did in the initializer list is still good.  But in the case where you changed `_i`, you don't recalculate, even though they are incorrect.

2. I'd recommend changing the limit to be on `cos(i)` rather than `i`.  The problem with `i` near pi/2 is that cos(i) == 0, so `inv_r0_cosi` is undefined.  Probably better to just set a minimum on `cosi` instead.

3. I don't think you need all the adjustments of the range then.  Just take the absolute value of cos(i) and tan(i).

4. Once `i` is switch to being an `Angle`, note that you can do the trig calls with `i.cos()` in lieu of `std::cos(i.rad())`.  It looks like we never wrote free functions `cos(i)`, etc. for `Angle` arguments.  That would be even nicer looking and of course easy to do, so if you feel like adding those, please go ahead.

In the limit of nu->infinity, the Spergel profile becomes a Gaussian.  That being the case, I don't think we need to have such a low upper limit for nu.  The practical limit is somewhere around nu = 150 or so, where xnorm start to approach 1.e323 where double precision will overflow.  So perhaps setting nu max to 100 would be reasonable.  I'm sure no one will consider this limit oppressive.  :)

It's not really wrong to have the doc string here, but it's kind of unnecessary.  Python won't display doc strings for private methods (leading underscore) when you say `help(ChromaticObject)` for instance.  It's generally sufficient to just use an in-code comment (hash comment) for private methods and functions.  And in that case, you don't need to include all the @param stuff when you are just documenting things for other developers.

If you change the meaning of `max_results` to mean the page size, then you'll break everyone who uses list_datasets, list_jobs, etc. who depends on its being the maximum number of values returned. You'll also break any other method in any client that uses `HTTPIterator` "as intended.".

You could argue that `max_results` is actually a more interesting arg than `page_size`, which is an implementation detail. (Most people probably just write a `for` loop over the iterator.) 
I guess you could also argue that if you only want `n` values, just stop looping when a counter reaches `n`. There is also a performance cost to that, though, when the batch size is larger than the remaining number of items you want to fetch.

So ideally both would be nice to have, but that's tricky given the current implementation of `HTTPIterator`, which effectively co-ops the API `max_results` query param to implement its meaning of `max_results`.

FWIW, in Go we don't have a `max_results` equivalent, only `page_size`.

The error in #2297 comes from the fact that the new `Batch` instance is pushed on first (`self._client._push_batch(self)`) and then `self.begin()` is called, which is where the API call (via `begin_transaction`) failed. So after that, the `Client`s stack is just stuck with that `Batch`.

The thing that makes it extra "bad" is that if the stack is not empty when it should be, calls like `Client.put()` will use that batch / transaction, instead of creating a new one. And those methods only `commit()` when they create their own `Batch` during the method call.

├── README.md
p2p/
├── src/
│   ├── something.py
│   ├── ...
├── setup.py
├── README.md
trinity/
├── src/
│   ├── something.py
│   ├── ...
├── setup.py
├── README.md

tox.ini
...
```

Then we'll set `RDY 0` on a connection even while it's still processing a message (https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/blob/master/nsq/reader.py#L664).  So we can end up in a situation where `total_rdy` is strictly less than the total count of in-flight messages.
